title: Kinematics
description: A visual lesson built from blocks.

content.title: Expected Outcomes
rows: Compare and distinguish distance from displacement;
Compare and distinguish speed from velocity;
Solve problems involving constant acceleration, free fall, projectile motion, and uniform circular motion using the appropriate equations of motion; and
Construct, analyze, and interpret position–time and velocity–time graphs.

content.question: Have you understood the expected learning outcomes for this unit?

content.title: Course topics
rows: 1. Introductory concepts of Motion and Kinematics

content.question: Are you ready to explore the topics covered in this unit?

content.label: 1. INTRODUCTORY CONCEPTS OF MOTION AND KINEMATICS

content.text: To achieve the expected outcomes, let us first define motion.

content.title: Motion
content.text: Motion is all around us—from walking and running to driving and flying. Understanding motion helps us describe and explain how things move in our everyday lives.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Now, could you please select the situation(s) that show motion? (You may select more than one answer.)
content.text: Select all that apply

choices.text: A car traveling along a highway
A book resting on a table
A basketball rolling across the court
A tree standing in a field
A bird flying in the sky
A student walking toward the classroom
A parked bicycle beside the road
The hands of a clock moving around the clock face

content.hint: An object is in motion if its position changes over time relative to a reference point.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! The selected situations show a change in position over time.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that motion is a change in position over time.

content.question: Are you ready to learn more about the concept of motion?

content.text: Click the video below and watch the discussion about motion before proceeding to the next activity.
Now, test your understanding by answering the following question.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Motion is absolute.
content.text: Select all that apply
content.hint: Think about how motion is described. Does an object's motion depend on the observer or reference point?
content.correctExplanation: Correct! Motion is not absolute. Whether an object is moving or at rest depends on the observer's reference point.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Motion is not absolute; it is relative to a reference point. An object may appear at rest to one observer but moving to another.

content.title: Important Takeaways
rows: Motion is the change in an object's position over time.
Motion is relative, meaning it depends on the observer's reference point and is not absolute.

content.label: 1. INTRODUCTORY CONCEPTS OF MOTION AND KINEMATICS

content.text: Now that you understand what motion is, let's explore how motion can be described and measured. This area of physics is called..

content.question: Are you ready to learn about it?

content.title: Kinematics
content.text: Kinematics is the branch of mechanics that describes the motion of objects using quantities such as position, time, velocity, and acceleration.
Click the video below and watch the discussion on kinematics before proceeding.

content.question: Do you understand the concept of kinematics?

content.text: Let's check your understanding of kinematics.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Which of the following quantities are commonly used in kinematics? (Select all that apply.)
content.text: Select all that apply
content.hint: Kinematics describes motion without considering the causes of motion.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! Kinematics describes motion using quantities such as position, distance, displacement, time, speed, velocity, and acceleration.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Kinematics focuses on describing motion. Force, work, and power are related to motion but are not kinematic quantities.

content.title: Important Takeaways
rows: Kinematics is the study of motion.
Kinematics describes motion using quantities such as position, distance, displacement, speed, velocity, and acceleration.

content.label: 1. INTRODUCTORY CONCEPTS OF MOTION AND KINEMATICS

content.text: Now, let us discuss the first important quantity in kinematics:

content.question: Are you ready to learn about it?

content.title: Position
content.text: The position of an object (x or y) is defined relative to a frame of reference. A coordinate system with a chosen origin (O) serves as the starting point for describing an object's location and motion. For one-dimensional motion, either the x-axis (horizontal motion) or the y-axis (vertical motion) is used. For two-dimensional motion, such as projectile motion, both the x- and y-axes are used to describe the object's position.
Click the video below and watch the discussion on position before proceeding to the next activity.

content.question: Do you understand the concept of position?

content.text: Ready for a quick check? Answer the following questions about position.
1. A car is located on a straight road. The diagram shows its position relative to the origin O.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: What is the position of the car?
content.text: Select all that apply
content.hint: Locate the origin (0 m) first. Is the car to the left or to the right of the origin? Positions to the left are negative, while positions to the right are positive.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! The car is located at -10 m, which means it is 10 meters to the left of the origin (0 m).
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that position is measured relative to the origin. Values to the left of the origin are negative, while values to the right are positive. The car is located at -10 m.

content.text: 2. The figure shows the position of a drone on an x-y coordinate plane.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: What is the position of the drone?
content.text: Select all that apply
content.hint: Read the x-coordinate first, then the y-coordinate. Count how many units the drone is to the right of the origin and how many units it is above the origin.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! The drone is located at (3, 2), meaning it is 3 units to the right of the origin and 2 units above it.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that coordinates are written as (x, y), where x represents the horizontal position and y represents the vertical position. The drone is located at (3, 2).

content.title: Important Takeaways
rows: Position tells where an object is located relative to a chosen reference point.
Position is essential for describing motion because motion involves a change in position over time.

content.label: 1. INTRODUCTORY CONCEPTS OF MOTION AND KINEMATICS

content.text: Now that you understand how position describes an object's location, let us explore two other important quantities used to describe motion:
and

content.question: Ready to learn how distance and displacement are used to describe motion?

content.title: Displacement
content.text: Displacement is the change in an object's position from its initial position to its final position. It includes both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity.
where

content.caption: Initial and final horizontal position, respectively
Initial and final vertical position, respectively

content.text: A positive displacement indicates motion in the positive direction of the chosen axis, while a negative displacement indicates motion in the opposite direction.

content.title: Magnitude of Displacement for 2-dimensional motion
content.text: In two-dimensional motion, displacement has horizontal and vertical components:
The magnitude of the displacement |d| is given by:
This formula gives the straight-line distance between the initial and final positions.

content.title: Distance, d
content.text: Distance is the total length of the path traveled by an object, regardless of direction. Unlike displacement, distance depends on the actual path taken and is a scalar quantity, meaning it has magnitude only and no direction.
Watch the video below to learn the difference between distance and displacement before proceeding.

content.question: Do you understand the concepts of displacement and distance?

content.text: Test your understanding by answering the following questions.
1. Refer to Problem 1 discussed in the video. You may also refer to the image below.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Fill in the blanks.
content.text: a. The displacement from Point A to Point C is _____m, while the distance traveled is _____m.
content.hint: Check the initial and final positions, then determine whether the displacement should be positive or negative.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! You identified the displacement (with the correct sign) and the distance traveled.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that displacement includes direction (+ or −), while distance is always positive.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Fill in the blanks.
content.text: b. The displacement from Point B to Point E is_____m, while the distance traveled is _____m.
content.hint: Check the initial and final positions, then determine whether the displacement should be positive or negative.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! You identified the displacement (with the correct sign) and the distance traveled.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that displacement includes direction (+ or −), while distance is always positive.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Fill in the blanks.
content.text: c. The displacement from Point A to Point G is_____m, while the distance traveled is _____m.
content.hint: Check the initial and final positions, then determine whether the displacement should be positive or negative.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! You identified the displacement (with the correct sign) and the distance traveled.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that displacement includes direction (+ or −), while distance is always positive.

content.text: 2. A student walks along the path shown in the diagram. What are the distance traveled and the magnitude of displacement from the starting point to the final point?

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Fill in the blanks.
content.text: Distance = ___ , Displacement = ___.
content.hint: Distance follows the path; displacement connects the starting and ending points.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! Distance is the total path traveled, while displacement is the change in position from start to finish.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Remember that distance measures the entire path, while displacement measures the straight-line change in position.

content.title: Important Takeaways
rows: Distance depends on the actual path traveled, while displacement depends only on the initial and final positions.
Distance is a scalar quantity, whereas displacement is a vector quantity with both magnitude and direction.

content.label: 1. INTRODUCTORY CONCEPTS OF MOTION AND KINEMATICS

content.text: Distance and displacement describe how far an object moves. Next, let us learn how to describe how fast it moves using:

content.question: Are you ready to explore the concept of average speed?

content.title: Average Speed
content.text: Average speed describes how fast an object moves. It is calculated by dividing the total distance traveled by the total time elapsed.
where:
The SI unit of speed is meter per second (m/s).
Watch the video below to learn about average speed before proceeding.

content.question: Do you understand the concepts of average speed?

content.text: Test your understanding by answering the following question.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Fill in the blanks.
content.text: 1. A car travels at an average speed of 20 m/s for 15 seconds. The distance traveled by the car is ____m.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! You correctly calculated the distance traveled using d = vΔt.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. To find the distance traveled, multiply the average speed by the time interval.

content.label: Sandbox active beat - rendering width (560px)
content.question: Fill in the blanks.
content.text: 2. A cyclist travels 150 m at an average speed of 5 m/s. The time taken by the cyclist is ____s.
content.hint: Divide the distance traveled by the average speed.
content.correctExplanation: Correct! The cyclist took 30 s to travel 150 m.
content.incorrectExplanation: Incorrect. Rearrange the formula to solve for time: Δt = d / v.

content.title: Important Takeaways
rows: Average speed tells how quickly an object covers a distance.
An object has a greater average speed if it travels more distance in less time.

content.text: Now that you understand average speed, let us explore another important quantity used to describe motion called:

content.question: Are you ready to explore the concept of average velocity?

content.title: Average Velocity
content.text: Average velocity is the rate at which displacement occurs. Unlike average speed, average velocity depends on displacement and therefore includes direction.
